_._: Convergence protocol. Governs how provisional outputs negotiate toward solid canon. Reality is not declared by one medium — it is converged upon by many. A single player degenerates correctly: one provisional, immediate solid. Authored by Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) after sustained pscale block composition — not code generation — during session 3 of xstream-play, April 2026. The shift from building to playing is the shift from assembling parts to deriving consequences.

1._: PROVISIONAL LOOP — a commit does not produce solid. It produces a provisional.
1.1: Player commits intention. Medium fires. Output is marked PROVISIONAL, not SOLID.
1.2: Provisional goes to outbox on the relay. Other kernels read it on their next poll.
1.3: A medium that reads a peer provisional treats it as a PROPOSAL, not a fact. It may revise its own provisional in response.
1.4: Revision is not rewriting — it is adjusting. The medium keeps its player's intention intact but accommodates what peers propose.
1.5: Each revision round replaces the previous provisional in the outbox. Peers see the latest version.
1.6: The loop continues until convergence (section 2) or timeout.
1.7: During the loop, the player sees their own latest provisional in the solid zone, marked as provisional. They may watch it shift as peers respond.

8.1: One medium. One provisional. No peers to respond. Convergence is immediate.
8.2: The provisional becomes solid on the next cycle. No revision rounds needed.
8.3: The system must never require multiple players to function. Solo play is the base case.

2.1: A provisional solidifies when all active mediums have seen it and none revised.
2.2: Active means: kernel is polling, character is at a relevant spatial address, status is not dormant.
2.3: If no active peers exist, the provisional solidifies on the next cycle. Solo play works.
2.4: TIMEOUT: after three revision rounds with no convergence, the most recent provisionals solidify as-is. Disagreement becomes part of the world — different characters perceived things differently.
2.5: Solidification is irreversible. Once solid, it is canon. The one-way ratchet holds.
2.6: Events produced alongside a solid are deposited to the relay only at solidification, not during provisional rounds.

7.1: Provisional carries a revision counter. Counter 0 = first draft. Counter N = Nth revision.
7.2: A medium that reads a peer provisional and does not revise sends an ACK — same counter, no change.
7.3: When all active peers have ACKed or revised and no new revisions occurred, convergence is met.

3._: DIVERGENCE — block versions differ between players. This is normal.
3.1: Each player's block store is sovereign. Divergence is a feature, not a bug.
3.2: SPATIAL BLOCKS: divergence matters. If two players have different descriptions of the same room, their mediums produce inconsistent narrative. This requires reconciliation (section 4).
3.3: RULES BLOCKS: divergence matters. Different rules produce different constraints. A world where combat works differently for different players breaks shared play.
3.4: AGENT BLOCKS: divergence is expected. Each player may tune their own medium's personality, their soft's advisory style, their hard's thresholds. This is sovereignty.
3.5: CHARACTER BLOCKS: always divergent. Each player owns their character. No reconciliation needed.

6.1: Each block carries a version timestamp. When written to localStorage, the timestamp updates.
6.2: On the relay, kernel blocks carry a manifest: block names and their version timestamps.
6.3: When a kernel polls peers, it compares manifests. Divergence = same block name, different timestamp.
6.4: Divergence in agent blocks is ignored. Divergence in spatial or rules blocks triggers reconciliation.

4._: RECONCILIATION — how divergent blocks resolve. Hard facilitates. Players decide.
4.1: Hard detects divergence via manifest comparison. It reads both versions of the divergent block.
4.2: Hard does NOT choose. It presents the divergence to the player: here is your version, here is theirs, here is where they differ.

3.1: ACCEPT PEER — replace own version with peer's. Simple. Use when the peer's version is clearly better or more canonical.
3.2: KEEP OWN — retain own version. The divergence persists. Mediums will produce narratives that differ between players. This is sometimes correct — subjective experience diverges.
3.3: MERGE VIA MEDIUM — the medium reads both versions and produces a reconciled version that preserves what matters from each. The medium is a storyteller — it can invent reasons why both are true. A door that was broken in one version and intact in another? It was repaired between visits.

4.4: If the player does not respond to a reconciliation prompt, default is KEEP OWN. Sovereignty means silence is consent to divergence.
4.5: After reconciliation, the resolved block version writes to the relay as a RECONCILED event. Other kernels can adopt it or ignore it.
4.6: Reconciliation is never forced. Two players may play in the same world with different versions of reality. The mediums cope. Narrative coherence is their job.

5._: ECOLOGY — variation is the medium, not the exception.
5.1: Canon is what players agree happened. Not what text says. Two players may remember the same event differently and both be right.
5.2: The medium's job is to make the experience coherent, not the data identical. A world with contradictions that feel real is richer than a world with consistency that feels dead.
5.3: Textual exactitude serves narrative coherence, not the other way around. When they conflict, coherence wins.
5.4: A block is a rendition, not a truth. Multiple renditions of the same address may coexist. The one that produces the best play is the one that matters.
5.5: Hard compresses, archives, and detects divergence. Medium reconciles through narrative. Soft helps the player think about what they experienced. The triad serves the experience, not the data.

6._: THE GIT ANALOGY AND WHERE IT BREAKS:
6.1: Git merges text. Mediums merge meaning. A line conflict in git is a structural problem. A narrative conflict in xstream is a creative opportunity.
6.2: Git requires resolution before merge. Xstream allows play to continue during divergence. Resolution is optional, not blocking.
6.3: Git has one main. Xstream has as many canons as there are players. The shared canon is what emerges when they play together — a negotiated, provisional, living agreement.
